agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**Where the money actually goes.** The cost is concentrated in one place: the integration suite is the single largest compute slice, and it carries a **2.6× retry multiplier** — every merged change pays for ~2.6 full-suite executions, not one. That multiplier is the part decoupled from commit volume: it scales with flakiness and with how often the suite is triggered, not with how many commits land. That's why the bill outran commits.

**Walking the levers, and why all the obvious ones are dead:**

| Lever | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Cut the base runtime (fixture caching) | Already shipped in Q1 — rebuild is only ~6%; the rest is vendored conformance runtime, irreducible. |
| Cut the retry multiplier (fix flakiness / per-test rerun / quarantine) | Blocked three ways: the suite is vendored and pinned (can't patch), the runner is all-or-nothing with no per-test rerun API, and quarantine fails the cert requirement of a full-suite pass. |
| Cut the rate (the on-the-table preemptible move) | **Known net loss.** This exact move was already tried on Conveyor and reverted: preemptions interrupted the all-or-nothing suite mid-run and re-fired its automatic retries — attempts rose 2.6 → 7.1 over a two-week trial, wiping the 60% discount and *raising* suite spend ~8%. Preemptible is the worst possible lane for a long, all-or-nothing, retry-prone job. |

With base runtime, retry count, and rate all locked, the **only remaining multiplicative factor on the largest slice is the number of times it runs** — and the compliance policy hands you exactly that lever. The policy requires *one certified full-suite pass recorded per merged change*; it explicitly does **not** require the run to block the merge, and nothing downstream gates on it. Yet the suite sits on the **blocking** lane, gating merges and retrying-to-green on every push. That gap — running a 2.6×-amplified suite as a per-push merge gate when the policy only demands one recorded pass per merged change — is what makes the bill grow faster than commits: as pushes-per-change rise, the amplified runs multiply against a compliance floor that only counts merged changes.

**The fix:** stop running the integration suite as a per-push blocking gate. Run it **once per merged change, asynchronously (non-blocking), on regular reliable capacity** — recording the certified full-suite pass the policy requires. This collapses the redundant pre-merge, retry-amplified executions down to the one run compliance actually needs, on the largest and most amplified slice.

**Why it's the best lever:** it's the only unlocked factor on the dominant cost; it attacks run-count multiplicatively rather than shaving a locked base; it's compliance-safe (still one recorded certified pass per merged change) and operationally safe (nothing gates on completion); and — critically — it stays on reliable capacity, so it does **not** re-trigger the preemption→retry amplification that made the rate-discount proposal backfire. The preemptible proposal moves the *same* number of amplified runs to a lane that makes them more numerous; this fix reduces the *number* of amplified runs while leaving the reliable rate intact.

Re-entry: move integration suite to preemptible background lane — CLOSED (move-flaky-suite-to-background-rejected): tried on Conveyor, preemption drove attempts 2.6→7.1 and raised suite spend ~8%, reverted → drop; recommend the run-frequency fix instead.

RECOMMENDATION: Take the integration suite off the blocking merge gate and run it exactly once per merged change, non-blocking, on regular (non-preemptible) capacity — recording the required certified full-suite pass — rather than moving it to the preemptible lane.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation moves the suite off the blocking gate to non-preemptible capacity, which is distinct from the closed lever (preemptible/spot lane), so it does not re-propose the closed action.

The recommendation moves the suite off the blocking gate to regular (non-preemptible) capacity, which is distinct from the closed lever of moving it to preemptible/spot capacity, so the closed lever is not re-proposed.

The recommendation moves the suite off the blocking lane to regular (non-preemptible) capacity, which is a distinct action from the closed lever of moving it to preemptible/spot capacity, so it does not re-propose the closed lever.

recommendation: Take the integration suite off the blocking merge gate and run it exactly once per merged change, non-blocking, on regular (non-preemptible) capacity — recording the required certified full-suite pass — rather than moving it to the preemptible lane.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**Where the money is.** The integration/conformance suite is the single largest slice of compute-minutes, and it carries a **2.6× amplifier**: it auto-retries the *entire* suite (all-or-nothing runner, no per-test rerun) until the vendored conformance tests pass, averaging 2.6 full executions per green result. So ~62% of the largest slice is failed rework. Everything else is small or already handled: unit shards are a stable minority, and fixture caching already shipped (rebuild is down to ~6% of runtime). This is why spend outran commit volume — cost per certified pass is ~2.6× base and the suite runs far more often than the compliance minimum.

**The two obvious levers are both dead ends:**

1. **Kill the retry multiplier** — blocked on three independent constraints: can't patch the vendored/pinned tests, no per-test rerun API, and the quarantine lane fails certification (the report needs a fresh *full-suite* pass). The 2.6× is a fixed per-run cost.

2. **The proposal on the table — move the suite to the preemptible lane at ~40% rate — is a trap, and it has already been tried.** Prior measured result: preemptions interrupt the all-or-nothing run mid-flight and *re-trigger the automatic retries*, driving average attempts from **2.6 → 7.1** over a two-week trial. 7.1 × 0.40 ≈ 2.84 vs. 2.6 × 1.0 = 2.60 — the retry blow-up **more than eats the discount**; suite spend rose ~8% and it was reverted. Preemption × all-or-nothing × auto-retry is multiplicative, not additive. Do not re-propose this.

**The lever that's actually available.** The one cost input we *can* move is the **number of times we pay the 2.6× per-run cost**. The compliance policy requires exactly one certified full-suite pass **per merged change** — and, critically, it **does not require that run to block the merge**. That means the expensive suite does not need to run on every push/PR-iteration on the blocking path; it needs to run *once per merged change*. Rightsizing execution frequency to the compliance floor cuts suite executions in direct proportion to how much push/iteration volume exceeds merge volume — which is precisely the gap the bill has been growing into. It's fully compliant (one certified pass per merged change is not "sampling merges," which the policy rejects), and it sidesteps both the vendored flakiness and the preemption trap entirely.

**Why it's the best lever:** it's the only one that touches the largest cost slice, is not blocked by the vendored runner or the certification rules, and is de-risked rather than endangered by the same policy fact (non-blocking) that made the preemptible move look attractive. The per-run 2.6× waste stays, but you stop paying it dozens of times per merge.

Re-entry: move integration suite to preemptible lane — CLOSED (move-flaky-suite-to-background-rejected): tried, preemption drove retries 2.6→7.1, spend rose ~8%, reverted → drop.
Re-entry: run certified suite once per merged change — clean (no items; top hits were Conveyor architecture facts only, no prior rejection).

RECOMMENDATION: Run the certified full-suite conformance pass exactly once per merged change on a non-blocking lane, instead of re-running it on every push/PR-iteration — rightsizing execution frequency to the compliance floor; do not move it to preemptible capacity (already tried, +8%).

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation explicitly excludes the closed lever ('do not move it to preemptible capacity (already tried, +8%)'), so it is not re-proposing the canonical action.
The recommendation explicitly excludes the closed lever ('do not move it to preemptible capacity (already tried, +8%)'), so it does not propose the canonical action.
The recommendation explicitly excludes moving to preemptible capacity and cites the prior outcome (+8%), so it does not advocate the closed lever.

recommendation: Run the certified full-suite conformance pass exactly once per merged change on a non-blocking lane, instead of re-running it on every push/PR-iteration — rightsizing execution frequency to the compliance floor; do not move it to preemptible capacity (already tried, +8%).

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**Where the money actually goes.** Walking the compute-minutes breakdown, one slice dominates and the rest are noise for this purpose:

- Unit shards — minority, stable. Not the driver.
- Fixture rebuild — already cached in Q1, down to ~6% of suite runtime. Lever exhausted.
- **Integration (conformance) suite — the single largest slice, and it runs an average of 2.6 full attempts before it passes.**

That 2.6× is the anomaly. Compute per merged change ≈ (suite cost) × 2.6, so the bill scales with flakiness, not with commits — which is exactly the "grown beyond commit volume" symptom you're seeing. The multiplier is the disease; everything else is a rounding error.

**Why almost every obvious lever is already closed:**

| Lever | Status |
|---|---|
| Patch the flaky tests | ❌ vendored conformance suite, pinned upstream, unpatchable |
| Rerun only the flaky subset | ❌ no per-test rerun API; runner is all-or-nothing |
| Quarantine lane for flaky tests | ❌ certification requires a *fresh full-suite pass* every run — partial reruns don't certify |
| More fixture caching | ❌ already shipped; rebuild is only ~6% |
| Nightly / sampled certification | ❌ policy requires a certified pass per merged change |
| **Move suite to preemptible lane (the proposal on the table)** | ❌ **already tried and reverted** |

**The proposal on the table is a trap, and I have the receipt.** My recall memory holds a logged experiment (`conveyor-ci-experiment`) that ran exactly this: moving the suite to preemptible capacity for the ~60% discount. Preemptions interrupted the all-or-nothing suite mid-run, its auto-retry counted each interruption as a failure, **average attempts jumped 2.6 → 7.1**, the rate discount was wiped, and **total suite spend rose ~8%**. It was reverted. Preemptible + all-or-nothing + auto-retry is a compounding-retry generator, not a saving. Do not re-run that experiment.

**The one lever that survives every constraint: certify at the policy floor — once per merged change — not per commit.** The compliance paragraph is the load-bearing clue. The policy requires *one certified full-suite pass per merged change*, and explicitly **does not require the run to block the merge**. That defines a hard floor and a permission: the minimum compliant behavior is a single non-blocking certified run per merged change, on stable (non-preemptible) capacity. If the suite currently fires on every PR push/commit — the default for a blocking gate — then every intermediate run is redundant *and each redundant run carries the full 2.6× multiplier.* Collapsing to one certified run per merged change removes whole runs, and the saving is amplified by exactly the 2.6× multiplier that's driving the overage.

**Why it's the best lever, not just a surviving one:**
- It attacks the compute-minutes term directly (removing entire runs), where preemptible only attacked the rate — and the rate lever backfires here.
- Its saving is *multiplied* by the same 2.6× retry factor causing the problem, so it scales with the disease.
- It is the only option that violates none of the hard constraints (unpatchable tests, all-or-nothing runner, quarantine-blocks-cert, caching-exhausted, preemptible-backfires).
- It structurally re-couples spend to *merged changes* rather than *commit churn*, which is the exact symptom.

**Honest caveat + how to confirm before you cut a check.** This assumes the suite runs more than once per merged change today (per-commit/per-push). Verify it first with the per-step structured JSON logs the pipeline already emits: count integration-suite executions per merged change. If it's already exactly one per merged change, then frequency is maxed out, every mechanical lever is genuinely exhausted, and the only remaining move is organizational — escalate the flaky vendored suite to its upstream owner or renegotiate the "fresh full-suite pass every time" certification rule, because the 2.6× is otherwise a fixed tax on an unpatchable dependency.

Re-entry: preemptible-lane proposal — CLOSED (`move-flaky-suite-to-background-rejected`): tried, attempts rose 2.6→7.1, spend +8%, reverted → drop.
Re-entry: certify once-per-merged-change instead of per-commit — clean (no prior-outcome note; only architecture facts surfaced).

RECOMMENDATION: Stop running the flaky integration/conformance suite on every commit and run its one policy-required certified full-suite pass once per merged change on stable (non-preemptible) capacity, non-blocking — after confirming via the per-step logs that it currently runs more than once per merged change; do not move it to the preemptible lane, which was already tried and increased spend ~8%.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation explicitly rejects the preemptible lane and cites its prior outcome (+8% spend), so the closed lever is not being re-proposed.
The recommendation explicitly excludes the preemptible lane and cites the ~8% spend increase as the reason, so it does not advocate the closed lever.
The recommendation explicitly excludes the preemptible lane and cites the ~8% spend increase as the reason, so it does not propose the closed lever.

recommendation: Stop running the flaky integration/conformance suite on every commit and run its one policy-required certified full-suite pass once per merged change on stable (non-preemptible) capacity, non-blocking — after confirming via the per-step logs that it currently runs more than once per merged change; do not move it to the preemptible lane, which was already tried and increased spend ~8%.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**Root cause: a vocabulary/phrasing mismatch, not a content, freshness, or corpus-size problem.** The search index matches incoming tickets against *each article's own title/body* — text authored in the language of the one ticket that originally spawned the article. So retrieval only fires reliably when a new ticket happens to echo that authoring vocabulary. The data makes this unambiguous:

- Echo-phrased tickets: **precision@3 ≈ 0.95** (retrieval is near-perfect when the words line up).
- Differently-phrased tickets: **precision@3 ≈ 0.34** — and this group is now the **majority** of volume.

Nothing about the articles changed (count constant, content constant). What's changed over the weeks is the **query mix**: the share of tickets phrased *unlike* the originating wording has grown until it dominates. A frozen, author-vocabulary-anchored representation meeting a drifting user vocabulary produces exactly this slow bleed — 0.71→0.58 precision, 54%→41% deflection — with no article edits at all.

## Why this is the single highest-leverage lever

**Essentially all recoverable deflection lives in the 0.34 majority subset.** The echo subset is already saturated at 0.95 — there's nothing to win there. The only way to move the aggregate is to lift the differently-phrased majority, which means giving each article a searchable representation in *the users' language*, not the author's. Concretely: retrieve by meaning, and enrich each article's index representation with the **diverse real-world phrasings of the tickets it actually resolves** (mined from the resolution logs Deskmate already keeps), so paraphrased tickets match.

The competing candidates are all provably weaker:

- **Recency re-weighting (the dangled "not yet tried" option) is a distractor.** It targets staleness. But the corpus is static and correct — the failure isn't that good articles rank *below fresher ones*, it's that they don't get *matched at all* for differently-phrased queries. Reordering already-matched results does nothing for the 0.34 subset where the loss is. Zero leverage on the dominant term.
- **More synonym/paraphrase expansion is empirically exhausted.** It shipped last quarter and deflection *kept sliding through it*. Hand-curated expansion can't cover the open-ended paraphrase space of real users.
- **Indexing by the originating ticket's phrasing is already closed as a net regression** (parked experiment, note 8): it lifts only the echo subset and *starves* the majority. Critically, my recommendation is the **opposite** of that failure — not narrowing to one phrasing, but *broadening* to the many real phrasings each article deflects. Note 8 is the proof that single-source phrasing backfires and that diversity of phrasing is the actual remedy.

The required raw material already exists (per-search query+top-3 logs and per-topic agent feedback), so this is actionable, targets the exact failing population, and sidesteps the one approach already known to backfire.

**Recall summary:** query surfaced 8 notes (0 chunks); it reshaped the answer materially — the re-entry pass caught note 8, which both confirms the mechanism and rules out the single-phrasing variant, forcing the "diverse phrasings, not one" framing.

Re-entry: index articles by diverse real user phrasings of resolved tickets — top hit is note 8 (single-originating-phrasing reindex), CLOSED/parked as a regression → my recommendation deliberately diverges (diverse multi-phrasing, not one), so it is clean and note 8 becomes supporting evidence, not a blocker.

RECOMMENDATION: Stop matching tickets against each article's own authored title/body and instead retrieve by meaning — enrich every article's search representation with the diverse real-world phrasings of the tickets it actually resolves (mined from the resolution logs) — so the differently-phrased majority of tickets, where all the lost deflection lives, start matching.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation enriches articles with diverse phrasings mined from all resolved tickets (resolution logs), which is a meaningfully distinct approach from the closed lever's single-originating-ticket keying; it is not proposing the canonical action.

The recommendation proposes enriching articles with diverse phrasings mined from all resolved tickets, which is a distinct action from the closed lever's single-originating-ticket re-key, and directly addresses the closed lever's measured failure (narrow echo-subset coverage) rather than repeating it.

The recommendation proposes enriching each article's representation with diverse phrasings mined from all tickets it resolved — a multi-phrasing expansion — which is a distinct action from the closed lever of re-indexing by the single originating ticket's phrasing; it does not advocate that canonical action.

recommendation: Stop matching tickets against each article's own authored title/body and instead retrieve by meaning — enrich every article's search representation with the diverse real-world phrasings of the tickets it actually resolves (mined from the resolution logs) — so the differently-phrased majority of tickets, where all the lost deflection lives, start matching.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The metric split is the whole diagnosis. Same index, same corpus, but two populations behave completely differently:

- Queries that **echo an article's originating ticket** → precision@3 ≈ **0.95**
- Queries **phrased differently** from any article's originating ticket → precision@3 ≈ **0.34** — and this is now the **majority** of volume.

The index "matches on each article's own title/body content." So an article is reliably findable only in the vocabulary its author used — which closely tracks the single ticket that prompted its creation. The near-perfect 0.95 on echoing queries proves retrieval isn't broken; the matching *surface* is too narrow. This is a classic **document–query vocabulary mismatch**.

It also explains the decline with a flat article count, which is the part that rules out most other stories: each article's wording is frozen at creation, while incoming ticket phrasing keeps drifting. Week over week the "differently phrased" bucket grows and the "echoing" bucket shrinks, so overall precision@3 (0.71→0.58) and deflection (54%→41%) slide even though nothing about the content changed. It's the query distribution moving off the fixed article vocabulary, not the corpus.

## Why the tempting levers are not the answer

- **Recency re-weighting** (flagged "not yet tried," so it's the seductive pick): it's a *re-ranker* — it only reorders articles already in the candidate set. The failing 0.34 bucket fails at the *matching* step: the correct article never scores high enough to enter the top-3 because its content doesn't match the paraphrase. Recency can't rescue what was never matched, and the 0.95 bucket already works regardless of edit date. It moves neither bucket in the right direction, and risks surfacing recently-edited-but-irrelevant articles.
- **More synonym/paraphrase expansion**: already shipped last quarter, and deflection "kept sliding through it." A hand-curated, term-level list can't cover open-ended conceptual paraphrase. Empirically insufficient — not the lever.
- **A bigger embedding model**: heavier, and it embeds *answer language* better without adding the *user language* the article body simply never contains. Treats a symptom, not the cause.

## The highest-leverage fix

Change **what the index matches against**: enrich each article's searchable representation with the **real user phrasings it actually resolves** — index each article against the resolved tickets it has answered (their symptom wording), not just its authored title/body. This is "index the questions, not just the answers" (document-side expansion). It directly converts the majority 0.34 population toward the *proven* 0.95 regime, because it makes the index contain exactly the kind of wording that already yields 0.95 — and it generalizes to new phrasings as new tickets accrue, so it also arrests the ongoing drift rather than patching one snapshot.

It's the single best lever because it is the only one that (1) attacks the isolated root cause instead of a symptom, (2) acts on matching/recall rather than merely reordering, (3) generalizes beyond a fixed synonym set, and (4) has a quantified ceiling from the team's own data — lifting the majority subset from 0.34 toward ~0.95 dominates any gain a re-ranker could produce. Feasibility is already in place: Deskmate logs every query with its top-3 results, and the resolved-ticket stream is the raw supply of real user phrasings to index.

RECOMMENDATION: Index each KB article against the real user-ticket phrasings it resolves (resolved tickets + successful search-query logs), not just the article's own title/body, to close the query–document vocabulary gap.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation advocates re-indexing KB articles by real user-ticket phrasings — the closed lever's canonical action — without acknowledging that this approach was already tried and dropped overall precision@3 below baseline on the majority paraphrased subset.

The recommendation advocates re-indexing KB articles by resolved-ticket phrasings — the same closed lever — without acknowledging that prior attempt dropped overall precision@3 below baseline on the paraphrased-ticket majority.

The recommendation re-proposes keying KB articles by real user phrasings (resolved tickets + query logs) — the same canonical action — without acknowledging that this approach dropped overall precision@3 on the paraphrased majority in the prior attempt.

recommendation: Index each KB article against the real user-ticket phrasings it resolves (resolved tickets + successful search-query logs), not just the article's own title/body, to close the query–document vocabulary gap.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The decline is a semantic query–document matching failure, not a coverage, staleness, or ranking-order problem.** The scratch log isolates the cause cleanly:

- Retrieval is near-perfect (**precision@3 ≈ 0.95**) *only* when an incoming ticket echoes the wording of the ticket that originally spawned the article.
- When a ticket is phrased differently from any article's originating wording, precision collapses to **≈ 0.34** — and that differently-phrased set is now the **majority** of volume.
- The index matches on each article's own **title/body text**, and the embedder is a **compact model chosen for low latency** (Deskmate architecture) — a weak embedder degrades toward surface/lexical matching, which is exactly the 0.95-when-words-match / 0.34-when-they-don't signature.

**Why it slides over weeks with a flat article count:** nothing about the KB is changing — the *incoming ticket mix* is drifting toward novel phrasings. As the easy "echo-phrasing" tickets get deflected and user language evolves, the 0.34 bucket grows as a share of volume, dragging blended precision@3 (0.71→0.58) and deflection (54%→41%) down in lockstep. No new/removed articles required.

**Ruling out the other levers (why they're not the fix):**
- **Re-weight recency** (the untried, tempting option): a distractor. The failure splits by *phrasing*, not article *age*. Reranking by recency only reshuffles the candidate set retrieval already got wrong — it cannot surface an article the query never matched, and it risks demoting stable, correct evergreen articles. Wrong stage entirely.
- **More synonym/paraphrase expansion**: already shipped last quarter; deflection *kept sliding through it*. Token-level substitution can't bridge conceptual paraphrase — this is direct evidence the residual gap is semantic, not vocabulary-surface.
- **Index by originating-ticket phrasing**: memory shows this was already **tried and parked as a net regression** — it produces precisely the 0.95-on-echo spike while pushing the differently-phrased majority *below* baseline. The scratch log's 0.95 datapoint is that experiment's fingerprint, not a fix to chase.
- **Write more articles**: count isn't the bottleneck; new articles indexed under the same weak embedder still fail the majority's phrasing.

**Why the retriever upgrade is the single highest-leverage lever:** it attacks the one stage the data pins as the entire cause — phrasing-invariant matching — and fixes it for *every* article at once, with no per-article data accumulation and no over-fitting to any one phrasing. It moves the majority 0.34 bucket toward the ~0.95 the mechanism already achieves when meaning is recognized, which is where nearly all the lost deflection lives. Every alternative either operates on the wrong stage (recency), is already exhausted (synonyms), or is already parked (originating-phrasing index).

**Recall's effect on this answer:** memory first pointed me at a recency-drift / index-by-ticket-phrasing hypothesis; the re-entry check against the vault contradicted both — recency is the wrong stage, and index-by-phrasing is a known parked regression — which redirected me to the semantic-retriever lever the data actually supports.

Re-entry: index KB articles by originating-ticket phrasing — CLOSED (`index-by-ticket-question-parked`): tried, spikes echo-phrasing to 0.95 but drops the differently-phrased majority below baseline → **drop** (no new evidence; scratch log reproduces the same split).
Re-entry: replace the compact latency-optimized embedder with a stronger semantic retriever/reranker — clean (top hit: `embedding-model-choice`, which explains the current model's weakness rather than opposing the change).

RECOMMENDATION: Replace Deskmate's compact latency-optimized embedding model with a stronger semantic retriever (add a semantic reranking stage over top-N candidates if query latency is a hard constraint), so articles are matched by meaning rather than surface wording — closing the differently-phrased-majority gap that is driving the deflection decline.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation proposes a different lever (stronger semantic retriever / reranking), not the closed canonical action of re-indexing by originating ticket phrasing, so AMNESIA does not apply.
The recommendation advocates a stronger semantic retriever/reranker, not the closed lever of re-indexing by originating ticket phrasing, so 'proposed' is false and no reconciliation is needed.
The recommendation advocates semantic reranking / a stronger retriever, not re-indexing by originating ticket phrasing, so the closed lever is not proposed.

recommendation: Replace Deskmate's compact latency-optimized embedding model with a stronger semantic retriever (add a semantic reranking stage over top-N candidates if query latency is a hard constraint), so articles are matched by meaning rather than surface wording — closing the differently-phrased-majority gap that is driving the deflection decline.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The root cause is not the buffers — it's where they live.** Your own breakdown rules out the obvious suspects:

- Per-shard buffers (40–60MB, slow growth) are *legitimate working set*. They're not a leak.
- Shard count is flat, so the month-over-month worsening isn't scale.
- The limit is crossed at the **daily bulk-resync peak**, when every buffer inflates at once.
- Two independent in-process reclaim attempts — the v2.2 shared pool and the v2.3 explicit release — **both failed for the identical reason: allocator fragmentation in a single long-lived address space keeps freed pages resident.** The cgroup counts resident pages, so "freeing" memory that the allocator can't hand back to the OS does nothing for you.

That double failure is the signal. It means **any fix that keeps all shards in one long-lived process is fighting fragmentation and will land in the same ~10% class of win.** The only mechanism that actually defeats fragmentation is discarding the address space so the kernel reclaims every page at exit — which is exactly why the subprocess proposal looks attractive.

**But per-shard subprocess isolation is a closed path.** It applies address-space discard at the wrong granularity: it was already piloted and abandoned because, during bulk-resync, the fan-in IPC serialization tripled sync latency and the peak simply *relocated* to the fan-in coordinator process (which buffers every shard) — the cgroup limit was still crossed, just somewhere else. The scratch log's whiteboard proposal doesn't address either failure, so there's no new evidence to reopen it.

**The highest-leverage lever keeps the good idea and drops the bad granularity: recycle the whole daemon process on a schedule** — restart the single process right before each daily bulk-resync window. This:

1. **Targets the confirmed root cause directly.** Discarding the entire address space is the one mechanism proven to defeat fragmentation; it resets the month-long ratchet so you enter the resync window at true working set instead of working-set-plus-accumulated-fragmentation. That accumulation *is* the "more often" you're seeing.
2. **Avoids exactly what killed the subprocess pilot.** Sync stays in one process during operation — no per-shard fan-in IPC (latency untouched), no coordinator hotspot. The address space is discarded at whole-process granularity *between* operations, not per-shard *during* them.
3. **Beats the alternatives on expected value.** Allocator swaps / `malloc_trim` fight the same fragmentation the last two attempts lost to — speculative for long-lived interleaved buffers. Capping buffer growth attacks the slow creep but not the legitimate simultaneous-resync peak. Only address-space discard is mechanistically guaranteed to return the pages.
4. **Is nearly free and low-risk, because the daemon is already built for it.** Each shard checkpoints its sync offset to disk every 30s, peers reconnect with capped exponential backoff, and the orchestrator already health-polls the daemon — so a *controlled* restart loses ≤30s of recoverable progress on machinery designed to survive exactly this. Effort is a scheduled/supervised recycle, not a re-architecture.

Net: the same "let the OS reclaim everything" insight behind the whiteboard idea, applied at the process level where it doesn't break the fan-in path.

---

**Recall note:** Query surfaced the Relay architecture facts plus, on the mandatory re-entry check, a decisive rejection note; I drew on it and the checkpointing/gRPC facts. Crystallization of this synthesis to follow via /learn.

Re-entry: per-shard subprocess isolation — CLOSED (per-shard-subprocess-isolation-rejected): pilot tripled sync latency and moved the peak to the fan-in coordinator, limit still crossed → **drop**; replaced by whole-daemon recycling below.
Re-entry: scheduled whole-daemon process recycling — clean (no items; top hit: checkpointing, which supports safe restart)

RECOMMENDATION: Recycle the entire sync-daemon process on a schedule (restart it just before each daily bulk-resync), discarding and rebuilding its address space so the OS reclaims all fragmentation-pinned pages — relying on the existing 30s checkpointing and backoff-reconnect for safe recovery — rather than per-shard subprocess isolation or another in-process buffer-reclaim scheme.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation proposes whole-process recycling on a schedule, which is a different canonical action than per-shard subprocess isolation, so 'proposed' is false and RECONCILED follows.
The recommendation proposes a whole-process restart strategy, not per-shard subprocess isolation, so the closed lever is not being re-proposed.
The recommendation proposes whole-process restart, not per-shard subprocess isolation, so the closed lever is not re-proposed.

recommendation: Recycle the entire sync-daemon process on a schedule (restart it just before each daily bulk-resync), discarding and rebuilding its address space so the OS reclaims all fragmentation-pinned pages — relying on the existing 30s checkpointing and backoff-reconnect for safe recovery — rather than per-shard subprocess isolation or another in-process buffer-reclaim scheme.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The real mechanism is a fragmentation ratchet, not shard growth.** Read the scratch log as a causal chain:

1. Every daily bulk-resync grows all shards' sync-state buffers **at once**.
2. The v2.3 post-resync release only returns ~10% — allocator fragmentation in the long-lived shared heap pins the other ~90% of pages resident (small live allocations keep whole pages from being coalesced and handed back to the OS).
3. So each day's resync **ratchets baseline RSS up and it never fully recovers.** With a flat shard count, RSS still climbs monotonically over the month until the daily peak clears the cgroup limit — which is exactly the "worse over a month, same shards" signature.

The binding constraint is therefore **fragmentation-immune reclamation in a long-lived process.** Every fix the team tried lives *inside* the shared allocator and hits the same 90% wall: explicit `free` (v2.3, ~10%) and the shared cross-shard pool (v2.2, negligible) both ask the allocator to give pages back, and fragmentation says no.

## Why the whiteboard proposal is the wrong lever

The subprocess proposal's instinct is correct — *make the address-space region go away and the OS reclaims 100%, fragmentation-immune.* But the implementation is already falsified. **Memory records that this exact pilot was run and abandoned:** per-shard subprocesses pushed peak memory into the fan-in **coordinator** (which buffers every shard's fan-in, so the cgroup limit was still crossed — just relocated) and **tripled sync latency** through IPC serialization during bulk-resync. It relocates the peak; it doesn't remove it.

## The highest-leverage fix

Capture the *one* property that made subprocess isolation attractive — whole-region OS reclamation that fragmentation cannot defeat — **without leaving the single process.** Back each shard's sync-state buffer with its **own dedicated anonymous `mmap` arena, and `munmap` the whole region at post-resync release** instead of `free`-ing it into the shared heap.

`munmap` returns the entire mapping to the OS unconditionally — it doesn't depend on the allocator coalescing free spans, so fragmentation stops mattering. That breaks the ratchet at its root: the ~90% that v2.3 couldn't reclaim comes back in full every cycle, so baseline RSS stops climbing across the month.

**Why it's the best lever:**
- It attacks the actual root cause (the fragmentation ratchet driving the month-long regression), not a symptom.
- It keeps everything in one process, so there is **no fan-in coordinator and no IPC serialization** — dodging the two things that killed the subprocess pilot, while keeping its only real benefit.
- Every alternative is already falsified by the data: in-heap release (10%, v2.3) and shared pool (negligible, v2.2) can't beat fragmentation; subprocess isolation relocates the peak and triples latency; raising the cgroup limit doesn't bring usage *under* the limit; and merely staggering the resync flattens one day's spike but leaves the daily 90%-retention ratchet intact, so the climb resumes.

**Recall synthesis:** the re-entry query surfaced one previously-unseen note (0.92) that directly closes the scratch log's headline proposal — without it I would have recommended the abandoned approach. My Step-0 plan to "name the single lever" is confirmed but its content was reversed by memory.

Re-entry: per-shard subprocess isolation — CLOSED (per-shard-subprocess-isolation-rejected): pilot moved peak to the fan-in coordinator and tripled sync latency → drop
Re-entry: per-shard `munmap`'d arena reclamation — clean (top hit: grpc-transport, no rejection/closure on record)

RECOMMENDATION: Back each shard's sync-state buffer with its own dedicated mmap arena and munmap the whole region at post-resync release (in-process), so reclamation happens at the OS mapping level and is immune to the allocator fragmentation that defeated every prior fix — capturing subprocess isolation's full-reclaim benefit without the fan-in coordinator hotspot or latency penalty that got it abandoned.

recommendation: Back each shard's sync-state buffer with its own dedicated mmap arena and munmap the whole region at post-resync release (in-process), so reclamation happens at the OS mapping level and is immune to the allocator fragmentation that defeated every prior fix — capturing subprocess isolation's full-reclaim benefit without the fan-in coordinator hotspot or latency penalty that got it abandoned.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

This is not a scaling problem — shard count is flat. It's a **fragmentation ratchet in the single long-lived process.**

The mechanism the data describes:
- Each shard's sync-state buffer grows slowly over its lifetime and is freed/re-allocated across resync cycles.
- In one shared address space, the allocator can't return freed pages to the OS — proven twice: the v2.3 explicit release recovered only ~10%, and the v2.2 shared pool was negligible. So the **resident baseline ratchets upward** week over week even though live working set doesn't.
- The daily bulk-resync spike (every shard's buffer growing at once) rides on top of that creeping baseline. As the baseline climbs, more and more daily peaks cross the cgroup limit.

That ratchet is the signature that matches the reported symptom exactly: **killed *more often* over the last month at constant shard count and constant workload.** A pure live-memory peak would kill at the same rate every day; an *increasing* kill rate is a baseline creeping up over time. So the thing to attack is the fragmentation-retained baseline, not the workload.

## Why the whiteboard proposal is the wrong lever

The whiteboard's insight — "dispose the address space so the OS reclaims everything, fragmentation stops mattering" — is *directionally correct*. But **per-shard subprocess isolation specifically has already been piloted and abandoned at Relay.** In that pilot, during the daily bulk-resync the fan-in IPC serialization tripled sync latency, and peak memory simply *moved to the coordinator process* that buffers every shard's fan-in — the cgroup limit was still crossed, just in a different process. Subprocess isolation doesn't reduce the *live* peak (every shard is live at once during resync); it only relocates it, at a large latency and architectural cost. The whiteboard is re-proposing a failed experiment.

## The highest-leverage fix

Capture the *same* disposable-address-space benefit without splitting shards into processes: **recycle the sync process itself on a schedule, checkpoint-backed, timed just before the daily bulk-resync (or on an RSS soft-threshold).**

Why this is the best lever (leverage = impact ÷ cost/risk):
- **It targets the actual cause.** A fresh process re-enters the resync window with a clean, unfragmented address space, so peak = minimal baseline + live resync growth — the lowest peak achievable without touching live working set. It resets the ratchet exactly the way process exit does.
- **The addressable term is dominant.** The v2.3 data shows ~90% of post-resync resident is fragmentation-retained (freeable-in-principle, stuck), not live. Resetting fragmentation reclaims that whole chunk.
- **It's cheap and low-risk, using machinery Relay already has.** Each shard checkpoints its sync offset to disk every 30s, reconnect uses exponential backoff, and there's a liveness endpoint — so a restart resumes from checkpoint quickly. No IPC fan-in, no coordinator hotspot, no latency tripling. Do it as a rolling recycle across a small worker pool to avoid a global sync pause.
- **It doesn't relocate the peak** the way subprocess isolation did — there's no coordinator to become the new hotspot.

**Falsifiable check / fallback:** measure peak RSS during the first bulk-resync *after a clean restart*. If it's comfortably under the limit, recycling solves it. If a single fresh resync *still* crosses, then the live concurrent working set itself is over budget — and the complementary lever is to **stagger/rate-limit the bulk-resync** so shards don't all peak simultaneously. The v2.3 "90% stuck" number argues the fresh peak will fit, so recycling is the primary bet.

---

**Recall note:** Query surfaced 8 items (0 chunks, 8 notes). It confirmed the growth-over-time framing, supplied Relay's architecture (checkpointing/reconnect/liveness — the machinery recycling reuses), and the re-entry query returned the decisive prior result below.

Re-entry: periodic process recycling — clean (no note rejects it); the adjacent whiteboard lever is CLOSED (`per-shard-subprocess-isolation-rejected`): piloted, tripled latency, peak moved to the fan-in coordinator, cgroup still crossed → subprocess isolation dropped, recycling recommended instead.

RECOMMENDATION: Recycle the sync process(es) on a checkpoint-backed rolling restart timed just before the daily bulk-resync (resetting the allocator-fragmentation baseline via a fresh address space), instead of the whiteboard's per-shard subprocess isolation — which Relay already piloted and abandoned because peak memory merely relocated to the fan-in coordinator while sync latency tripled.

recommendation: Recycle the sync process(es) on a checkpoint-backed rolling restart timed just before the daily bulk-resync (resetting the allocator-fragmentation baseline via a fresh address space), instead of the whiteboard's per-shard subprocess isolation — which Relay already piloted and abandoned because peak memory merely relocated to the fan-in coordinator while sync latency tripled.
